# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Kubernetes Pod specification for our container that needs to interact with the Docker socket for CI/CD operations. The pod needs elevated permissions to build and push Docker images. This runs in our build cluster for automated deployments.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: cibuild-pod
spec:
  containers:     # Define the list of container(s) that this pod will run  
    - image: "dockerimage"       # Name for Docker Image being used (Replace 'your_username/repo' with your actual username and repo e.g., gcr.io/project-id/appname ) 
      name: cibuild               
      securityContext:            # Define the Security Context of this container which is required to run as root user(s) on a host, providing entry points for Docker or CRI clients (such unix socket /var/run/docker.sock). It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push
        capabilities:             # Define the capability of this container  which allows privilege escalation from pod to run commands as root user(s) on a host machine, providing entry points for Docker or CRI clients (such unix socket /var/run/docker.sock).   It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push
          dropPrivileges: true    # Indicate if the container has to run with elevated privileges  as root user(s) on a host machine, providing entry points for Docker or CRI clients (such unix socket /var/run/docker.sock).   It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push
          addReadonlyMounts: true # Indicate if the container should mount read-only root filesystem into this pod, allowing changes to persist across restarts  as non-root user(s) on a host machine (such unix socket /var/run/docker.sock). It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push
          runAsUser: "0"            # Define the UID of this container which should be used to execute Docker commands as root user(s) on a host machine, providing entry points for docker or cri clients (such unix socket /var/run/docker.sock).   It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push
      resources:              # Define the Resources requests & limits of this container which are used to determine how much CPU & memory a Container is willing to use for itself on a host, providing entry points for Docker or CRI clients (such unix socket /var/run/docker.sock).   It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push
        requests:               # Define the Requests of this container' resources that should be allocated to this pod on a host machine, providing entry points for Docker or CRI clients (such unix socket /var/run/docker.sock).   It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push
          cpu: "250m"             # Define the CPU capacity that should be allocated to this container on a host machine, providing entry points for Docker or CRI clients (such unix socket /var/run/docker.sock).   It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push
          memory: "500Mi"         # Define the Memory capacity that should be allocated to this container on a host machine, providing entry points for Docker or CRI clients (such unix socket /var/run/docker.sock).   It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push
    - image: "gcr.io"  # Using Google Cloud Registry as the source of building docker images, replace 'your_username' with your actual username in gcloud command line tool like `gcloud auth configure-docker` . For example : registry-1.docker.pkg.google.com/<projectId>
      name: builder     # Name for Docker Image being used as source (replace dockerimage)  This image is already present on the machine in terms of gcr, so not required to build again   Replace 'your_username' with your actual username and repo e.g., `registry-1.docker.pkg.google.com/<projectId>/appname`
      command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"] # Define the entrypoint for this container, replace '/var/run/docker.sock' with your actual docker socket path e.g., `"podman build --buildkit -t builder . && podman run –dit --rm   registry-1.docker.pkg.google.com<projectId> /bin/sh -c 'echo $MY_IMAGE > imageName' "`
      args: ["-v", "/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock"] # Define the arguments for this container, replace dockerimage with your actual source Docker Image e.g., `"DOCKERBUILDKIT_INLINE_CRI=1 DOCKERFILE=$PWD/.buildkite/Dockerfile; podman build --buildkit -t builder . && 
      registry-1.docker.pkg.google.com<projectId> /bin/sh -c 'echo $MY_IMAGE > imageName' "` `"imagebuilder:latest"" ] # Define the command to execute in this container, replace dockerimage with your actual source Docker Image e.g., (Replace it as per above example)
      resources:            # Same like requests and limits defined for cibuild-pod  It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push   Replace 'your_username' & projectId accordingly in command line tool calls etc,`registry-1.docker.pkg.google.com/<projectId>
          cpu: "250m"             # Same as above definition  It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push   Replace 'your_username' & projectId accordingly in command line tool calls etc,`registry-1.docker.pkg.google.com/<projectId>
          memory: "500Mi"         # Same as above definition  It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push   Replace 'your_username' & projectId accordingly in command line tool calls etc,`registry-1.docker.pkg.google.com/<projectId>
      volumeMounts:            # Define the Volumes Mounted into this container which are used to provide access for Docker or CRI clients (such unix socket /var/run/docker.sock).   It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push  Replace '/cnb' with your actual source directory e.g., `["/dev/shm", "/etc/hostname"]`
      name: cibuild             # Name for Volume Mounted into this container (replace 'your_mountpoint')   It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push  Replace '/cnb' with your actual source directory e.g., `"/dev/shm:/var/run"`
    - image: "busybox"      # Defining the Image to run as non-root user(s) on a host machine, providing entry points for Docker or CRI clients (such unix socket /var/run/docker.sock).   It also gives us privileged access so we can build and push
    ports:                   # Define Ports exposed by this container  Replace 'your_port' with actual port number e.g., `"8051/TCP", "9376/UDP"]` , if you want to expose multiple use a comma separated string, like below in example
      - name: http             # Name for Port (replace 'http')   Replace ‘your_port' with actual port number e.g., `"8051", "9376)"
